examples.title: Binary Search Recursive
examples.code:
FUNCTION BinarySearch(arr, target, low, high)
 IF low > high THEN
 RETURN -1 // Base case: not found
 ENDIF
 
 mid = (low + high) / 2
 
 IF arr[mid] = target THEN
 RETURN mid // Base case: found
 ELSE IF arr[mid] < target THEN
 RETURN BinarySearch(arr, target, mid + 1, high)
 ELSE
 RETURN BinarySearch(arr, target, low, mid - 1)
 ENDIF
END FUNCTION

// Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
// Solution: O(log n)
examples.explanation: Each call halves the search space. Work per call is constant (comparing and calculating mid). Recursion depth is log n.

examples.title: Merge Sort
examples.description: Divide and conquer sorting showing T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)
examples.explanation: Problem divided into two equal halves, solved recursively, then merged in linear time. Total work at each level is n, with log n levels.

examples.title: Naive Fibonacci (Inefficient)
examples.description: Demonstrates exponential time from repeated subproblems
examples.explanation: Each call generates two more calls until reaching base cases. Massive redundancy - same values calculated exponentially many times. Use dynamic programming instead.

examples.title: Tower of Hanoi Solver
examples.description: Classic recursive problem with exponential complexity
examples.explanation: Each disk requires double the moves of the previous disk. For n=64, requires 2^64-1 ≈ 1.8×10^19 moves - thousands of years!

examples.title: QuickSort
examples.description: Recursive sorting with best/worst case analysis
examples.code:
FUNCTION QuickSort(arr, low, high)
 IF low < high THEN
 pivotIndex = Partition(arr, low, high)
 
 // Pivot in correct position
 QuickSort(arr, low, pivotIndex - 1) // T(k)
 QuickSort(arr, pivotIndex + 1, high) // T(n-k-1)
 ENDIF
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Partition(arr, low, high)
 pivot = arr[high]
 i = low - 1
 FOR j = low TO high - 1 DO
 IF arr[j] <= pivot THEN
 i = i + 1
 SWAP arr[i], arr[j]
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
 SWAP arr[i+1], arr[high]
 RETURN i + 1
END FUNCTION

// Worst case: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) = O(n²)
// Best case: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = O(n log n)
examples.explanation: Partition splits array. Worst case when pivot is always smallest/largest (unbalanced). Best case when pivot splits evenly. Average case O(n log n).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst
